text: Two standard thoracoscopic ports are visible on the patient's chest, providing access for the thoracoscopic instruments during the procedure.
The surgeon inspects the right hemithorax to identify areas of redundant diaphragm tissue, observing the elevated right hemidiaphragm.
The surgeon places a linear clamp across the redundant diaphragm in the right hemithorax, preparing for further manipulation or resection.
The surgeon carefully applies tension to sculpt the contour of the diaphragm for the staple line, ensuring precise alignment and positioning during the procedure.
The surgeon places a linear stapler beneath the cross clamp on the redundant diaphragm and advances the staple line medially, securing the tissue.
The surgeon revises the placement of the cross clamp after each staple load, ensuring precise alignment along the staple line in the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon adjusts the tension and contour of the diaphragm plication, ensuring the staple line is properly sculpted and accommodates the redundant diaphragm in the right hemithorax.
The surgeon completes the staple line medially, ensuring the linear stapler advances along the designated path beneath the cross clamp.
The surgeon removes the specimen and inspects the peritoneal surface of the diaphragm, checking for any abnormalities or ensuring proper surgical outcomes.
The surgeon over-sews the staple line, reinforcing the previously placed staples along the surgical site.
The surgeon inspects the placation for proper tension and contour, ensuring the surgical site is appropriately aligned and secured.
